plainMeaning: The poet now turns from the heavens to our own earth and begins naming its great Jain pilgrimage mountains — Aṣṭāpada, Sammeta-śikhara, Girnār, Śatruñjaya, Gajapada, the Dharma-wheel shrine and the towering Vaibhāra hill.
recitation: (ḍhāḷa āṭhamī) aṭṭhāvaya sameta-śikhara giri, sā-janaji a | revata-giri settuṁja || gajapada dhammacakka kahuṁ. sā(-janaji a) | vebhāra giri uttuṁga

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati ḍhāḷa 'tune, melodic movement of a poem' + āṭhamī 'eighth' (from Sanskrit aṣṭama-).
wordByWord.notes: A structural rubric, not part of the chanted line; it marks the start of the tīrtha-garland section.

wordByWord.gloss: Aṣṭāpada (the mountain where Ṛṣabha attained liberation)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit aṭṭhāvaya < Sanskrit aṣṭāpada, lit. 'eight-stepped'; the holy peak associated with the first Jina's nirvāṇa.
wordByWord.notes: Place-name; the Prakrit doubling -ṭṭh- and -y- glide are the book's orthography.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit sammeta- + śikhara 'peak'; the hill where twenty of the twenty-four Tīrthaṅkaras reached mokṣa.
Sanskrit/Prakrit giri 'mountain', carried unchanged into Prakrit (Sheth, Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo).
wordByWord.notes: Here qualifies Sammeta-śikhara; the word recurs below with Vaibhāra.

wordByWord.gloss: O good souls! (the dhāla's refrain, addressing the virtuous devotees)
wordByWord.etymology: sā(jjana) < Sanskrit sajjana 'virtuous person' + honorific -ji + vocative particle a; the printed variant is 'સાજનજિ અ'.
wordByWord.notes: This is the ṭeka (refrain) abbreviated elsewhere as 'સા૦'. Its exact segmentation is editorial; some reciters hear 'sā jaṇa-ji a' = 'O dear good folk'.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit raivata- (Mount Raivataka) + giri 'mountain'; the Girnār massif near Junagadh.
wordByWord.notes: Place-name; sacred especially to the 22nd Jina Neminātha.

wordByWord.gloss: Śatruñjaya (Palitana hill, chief tīrtha of Ādinātha)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit settuṁja < Sanskrit śatruñjaya 'conqueror of foes (i.e. inner enemies)'.
wordByWord.notes: Place-name; the spelling settuṁja is the book's verbatim Prakrit form.
A lesser-documented tīrtha; identifications vary (linked in some traditions to the Vāsupūjya / Champā region). Marked uncertain.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit dhammacakka < Sanskrit dharmacakra 'wheel of the Law'; here a named tīrtha bearing that emblem.
wordByWord.notes: Often associated with the ancient Dharmacakra tīrtha (traditionally Takṣaśilā/Taxila); identification is traditional, not asserted as certain.

wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati/Apabhraṁśa kahuṁ, 1st-person present < Prakrit kahaï < Sanskrit kathayati 'tells'.
wordByWord.notes: The poet's first-person voice introducing the litany of holy places.

wordByWord.etymology: Scribal abbreviation (sā + daṇḍa) standing for the full refrain સાજિનજિઅ above.
wordByWord.notes: The book marks the recurring ṭeka by its first syllable; the reciter restores the whole refrain.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit vebhāra < Sanskrit vaibhāra; one of the five hills girdling ancient Rājagṛha in Magadha.
wordByWord.notes: Place-name; site of Jina sermons (Munisuvrata/Mahāvīra tradition).

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit/Sanskrit uttuṅga, ut- 'up' + tuṅga 'high, elevated' (Wisdom Library; Sheth).
wordByWord.notes: Epithet of Vaibhāra-giri, ending the line on an image of height.

literalTranslation: (Eighth dhāla.) Aṣṭāpada, Sammeta-śikhara mountain — O good souls! — Raivata-giri, Śatruñjaya; Gajapada, Dharmacakra I recite — O good souls — the lofty Vaibhāra mountain.
idiomaticTranslation: Now hear, O virtuous ones, this garland of holy peaks: Aṣṭāpada and Sammeta-śikhara; Girnār and Śatruñjaya; Gajapada and the Dharma-wheel shrine I name aloud — and the lofty hill of Vaibhāra too.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Having swept the worshipper's gaze through the temples and images of the three worlds, the poet now lets it settle on this very earth, and the eighth dhāla opens as a tīrthamālā — a literal 'garland' threaded mountain by mountain. The selection is not random: each named peak is a mokṣa-bhūmi or a kalyāṇaka-hallowed site. Aṣṭāpada carries the nirvāṇa of the first Jina Ṛṣabha; Sammeta-śikhara holds the liberation of twenty Tīrthaṅkaras; Raivata-giri (Girnār) is the mountain of Neminātha; Śatruñjaya is Ādinātha's pre-eminent tīrtha. To these the poet adds Gajapada and the Dharma-wheel shrine, and crowns the line with Vaibhāra-giri of Rājagṛha, marked uttuṁga, 'towering'. The verb kahuṁ ('I recite') is significant: the poet steps forward in the first person, no longer counting cosmic shrines but personally calling out earthly names, drawing the listener into a shared act of remembrance. The refrain sā-janaji a ('O good souls') turns the litany outward to the congregation, so that naming becomes a communal summons. Where dhālas 5–7 overwhelmed with lakhs and crores, this verse works by the intimacy of proper names every Jain knows — the rhythm shifts from awe at number to warmth of recognition, and the towering close on uttuṁga keeps the upward, ascending motion that has run through the whole hymn.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on sthāvara-tīrtha bhakti: the Jain conviction that places sanctified by a Tīrthaṅkara's kalyāṇakas — above all the nirvāṇa-kalyāṇaka — become enduring fields of merit whose very darśana strengthens samyag-darśana (right faith). Naming a mokṣa-bhūmi is itself an act of homage that aligns the reciter's resolve with those who crossed over there. The closing epithet uttuṁga also carries a quiet doctrinal resonance: the physical height of the tīrtha mirrors the spiritual elevation (ūrdhva-gati) of the liberated soul.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited at dawn, the verse lets the practitioner make a mānasika yātrā — a mental pilgrimage — bowing in turn to Aṣṭāpada, Shikharji, Girnār, Śatruñjaya and Vaibhāra without leaving the upāśraya, gathering a measure of pilgrimage-merit and steadying the day's faith. The refrain invites doing this not privately but as a community, calling fellow 'good souls' to remember the holy places together.

crossReferences.text: Aṣṭottarī Tīrthamālā (108 tīrthas) — the gaccha's own pilgrimage-litany recited in Pakkhī
Nirvāṇa-bhūmis of the Tīrthaṅkaras (Aṣṭāpada, Sammeta-śikhara, Girnār, etc.)

sources.title: Uttunga, Uttuṅga — definitions
sources.consultedFor: Gloss and etymology of uttuṁga 'lofty, high' (ut- + tuṅga).

sources.title: Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo (Sheth) — Comprehensive Prakrit Dictionary
sources.consultedFor: Prakrit forms giri, uttuṁga, settuṁja and verb root kah-.

sources.title: Shikharji (Sammeta-śikhara)
sources.consultedFor: Identification of Sammeta-śikhara as the nirvāṇa site of twenty Tīrthaṅkaras.

sources.title: Shatrunjaya
sources.consultedFor: Identification of Śatruñjaya (Palitana) as the chief tīrtha of Ādinātha.

sources.title: Girnar
sources.consultedFor: Identification of Raivata-giri (Girnār) and its link to Neminātha.

notes: Verse-specific caveats: (1) The refrain is printed both as 'સાજિનજિઅ' and parenthetically as 'સાજનજિ અ', and abbreviated mid-stanza as 'સા૦'; I read it as sā(jjana)-ji a, 'O good souls', but the precise segmentation is editorial. (2) 'ગજપદ' (Gajapada/Gajāgrapada) and 'ધમ્મચક્ક' (Dharmacakra) are tīrthas with variant traditional identifications — Gajapada is sometimes placed in the Champā/Vāsupūjya region and Dharmacakra is traditionally linked to Takṣaśilā (Taxila); neither is asserted with certainty. (3) Prakrit spellings (aṭṭhāvaya, settuṁja, vebhāra, uttuṁga) are preserved verbatim per the Anchalgachchha (Vidhipaksha) edition's orthography. (4) The bracketed heading '(ઢાળ-આઠમી)' is a structural rubric, not a chanted token.
